name: praetorian-cli

metadata.name: praetorian-cli
metadata.summary: For interacting with the Chariot API

documentation:
# Praetorian CLI and SDK [![Python Version](https://img.shields.io/badge/Python-v3.9+-blue)](https://www.python.org/) [![pip Version](https://img.shields.io/badge/pip-v23.0+-blue)](https://pypi.org/project/praetorian-cli/) [![License](https://img.shields.io/badge/License-MIT-007EC6.svg)](LICENSE) [![Contributor Covenant](https://img.shields.io/badge/Contributor%20covenant-2.1-007EC6.svg)](CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md) [![Open Source Libraries](https://img.shields.io/badge/Open--source-%F0%9F%92%9A-28a745)](https://opensource.org/) [![Contributions Welcome](https://img.shields.io/badge/Contributions-Welcome-brightgreen.svg?style=flat)](https://github.com/praetorian-inc/chariot-ui/issues) :link: [Chariot Platform](https://chariot.praetorian.com) :book: [Documentation](https://docs.praetorian.com) :bookmark: [PyPI](https://pypi.org/project/praetorian-cli/) # Table of Contents - [Description](#description) - [Getting started](#getting-started) - [Prerequisites](#prerequisites) - [Installation](#installation) - [Signing up](#signing-up-and-configuration) - [Using the CLI](#using-the-cli) - [Using scripts](#using-scripts) - [Developers](#developers) - [Contributing](#contributing) - [Support](#support) - [License](#license) # Description Praetorian CLI and SDK are open-source tools for interacting with our products and services. Currently, they support access to [Chariot](https://www.praetorian.com/proactive-cybersecurity-technology/), our offensive security platform.
The SDK exposes the full set of APIs that the Chariot UI uses.
The CLI is a fully-featured companion to the Chariot UI. # Getting Started ## Prerequisites - Python v3.9 or above - pip v23.0 or above ## Installation Install the Python package using this command: ```zsh pip install praetorian-cli ``` ## Signing up and configuration 1. Register for an account for [Chariot](http://chariot.praetorian.com) using the instructions in [our documentation](https://docs.praetorian.com/hc/en-us/articles/25784233986587-Account-Setup-and-Initial-Seeding). 2. Run `praetorian configure` and follow the prompts. 3. It creates `~/.praetorian/keychain.ini`, which should read like this: ``` [United States] name = chariot client_id = 795dnnr45so7m17cppta0b295o api = https://d0qcl2e18h.execute-api.us-east-2.amazonaws.com/chariot username = lara.lynch@acme.com password = 8epu9bQ2kqb8qwd.GR ``` For more advanced configuration options, as well as SSO. See [the documentation on configuration](https://github.com/praetorian-inc/praetorian-cli/blob/main/docs/configure.md). # Using the CLI The CLI is a command and option utility for access to the full suite of Chariot API. See documentation for commands using the `help` option: ```zsh praetorian chariot --help ``` As an example, run the following command to retrieve the list of all assets in your account: ```zsh praetorian chariot list assets ``` To get detailed information about a specific asset, run: ```zsh praetorian chariot get asset ``` # Using scripts The CLI has a scripting engine for implementing more complex workflows. They add end-to-end functions as commands grouped under `script`. To see a list of them: ```zsh praetorian chariot script --help ``` For example the following command is used to ingest scan results from Nessus XML export files: ```zsh praetorian chariot script nessus-xml ``` You can find the list of scripts that comes with the CLI in [this directory](https://github.com/praetorian-inc/praetorian-cli/tree/main/praetorian_cli/scripts/commands) ## Loading more extension scripts In addition to scripts that are packaged with the CLI, you can point CLI to directories with scripts to further extend the CLI with those scripts. Set the `PRAETORIAN_SCRIPTS_PATH` environment to point to directories where you store additional extension scripts. # Developers Both CLI and SDK is open-source in this repository. The SDK is installed along with the `praetorian-cli` package. You can extend Chariot by creating scripts using the SDK. ## SDK Integrate the SDK into your own Python application with the following steps: 1. Include the dependency ``praetorian-cli`` in your project. 2. Import the Chariot class ``from praetorian_cli.sdk.chariot import Chariot``. 3. Import the Keychain class ``from praetorian_cli.sdk.keychain import Keychain``. 4. Call any function of the Chariot class, which expose the full backend API. See example below: ```python from praetorian_cli.sdk.chariot import Chariot from praetorian_cli.sdk.keychain import Keychain chariot = Chariot(Keychain()) chariot.add('asset', dict(name='example.com', dns='example.com')) ``` The best place to explore the SDK is the code of the CLI, especially [the handlers of the CLI](https://github.com/praetorian-inc/praetorian-cli/tree/main/praetorian_cli/handlers) You can inspect the handler code to see how each CLI command is implemented with the SDK. ## Developing scripts If you want to take advantage of the scaffolding of the CLI, you can write fully fledged functions using the scripting engine. For developing scripts, you can refer to this [readme file](https://github.com/praetorian-inc/praetorian-cli/blob/main/docs/script-development.md). ## Contributing We welcome contributions from the community, from scripts, to the core CLI and SDK. To contribute, fork this repository and following the [GitHub instructions](https://docs.github.com/en/get-started/exploring-projects-on-github/contributing-to-a-project) to create pull requests. By contributing, you agree to our [Code of Conduct](CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md). ## Support If you have any questions or need support, please open an issue [here](https://github.com/praetorian-inc/chariot-ui/issues) or reach out via [support@praetorian.com](mailto:support@praetorian.com). ## License This project is licensed under the MIT License - see the [LICENSE](LICENSE) file for details.

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.test.test
documentation: Run integration test suite

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.get.get
documentation: Get entity details from Chariot

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.get.asset
documentation:
Get asset details

 
Argument:
    - KEY: the key of an existing asset

 
Example usages:
    - praetorian chariot get asset "#asset#api.example.com#1.2.3.4"
    - praetorian chariot get asset "#asset#api.example.com#1.2.3.4" --details

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.get.risk
documentation:
Get risk details

 
Argument:
    - KEY: the key of an existing risk

 
Example usages:
    - praetorian chariot get risk "#risk#api.example.com#CVE-2024-23049"
    - praetorian chariot get risk "#risk#api.example.com#CVE-2024-23049" --details

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.get.attribute
documentation:
Get attribute details

 
Argument:
    - KEY: the key of an existing attribute

 
Example usage:
    - praetorian chariot get attribute "#attribute#source#kev#risk#api.example.com#CVE-2024-23049"

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.get.account
documentation:
Get account (collaborator or authorized master account) details

 
Argument:
    - KEY: key of an existing account

 
Example usage:
    - praetorian chariot get account "#account#peter@example.com#john@praetorian.com"

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.get.integration
documentation:
Get integration details

 
Argument:
    - KEY: key of an existing integration connection

 
Example usage:
    - praetorian chariot get integration "#account#john@praetorian.com#azure#556bee78-30d0-4a4c-8e4f-8ac2e19ce3d5"

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.get.job
documentation:
Get job details

 
Argument:
    - KEY: key of an existing, recently scheduled, job

 
Example usage:
    - praetorian chariot get job "#job#api.example.com#1.2.3.4#portscan"

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.get.file
documentation:
Download a file using key or name

 
Argument:
    - NAME: key or name of an existing file

 
Example usage:
    - praetorian chariot get file "#file#proofs/example.azurewebsites.net/jira-unauthenticated-user-picker"
    - praetorian chariot get file "proofs/example.azurewebsites.net/jira-unauthenticated-user-picker"
    - praetorian chariot get file "proofs/example.azurewebsites.net/jira-unauthenticated-user-picker" --path ~/Downloads

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.get.definition
documentation:
Download a definition using the risk name

 
Argument:
    - NAME: name of a risk

 
Example usage:
    - praetorian chariot get definition jira-unauthenticated-user-picker
    - praetorian chariot get definition CVE-2024-23049

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.get.webhook
documentation:
Get the webhook URL

 
Example usage:
    - praetorian chariot get webhook

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.link.link
documentation: Add a collaborator to your account

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.link.account
documentation:
Add a collaborator account to your account

This allows them to assume access into your account
and perform actions on your behalf.

 
Arguments:
    - NAME: their email address



 
Example usages:
    - praetorian chariot link account john@praetorian.com

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.search.search
documentation:
Query Chariot for matches or counts using the search syntax

 
Search syntax:

 
- Search by prefix of the key of the entries:
    - "#asset#www"
    - "#asset#www.example.com#12.1."
    - "#risk#api.example.com"
 
- Search by prefix of DNS:
    - "dns:www.example.com"
    - "dns:www."
    - "dns:https://github.com/praetorian-inc/praetorian-cli"

 
- Search by prefix of IP address:
    - "ip:12.12.1."

 
- Search by prefix of name:
    - "name:CVE-2024-"

 
- Search by prefix of status:
    - "status:OH"
    - "status:JF"
    - "status:AH"
    - See https://github.com/praetorian-inc/praetorian-cli/blob/main/docs/terminology.md for list of all statuses

 
- Search by prefix of source:
    - "source:#asset#www.example.com#"
    - "source:#risk#www.example.com#"

 
Example usages:
    - praetorian chariot search --term "status:OH"
    - praetorian chariot search --term "status:OH" --details --page all
    - praetorian chariot search --term "#asset#www.example.com"
    - praetorian chariot search --term "dns:https://github.com/praetorian-inc/"

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.add.add
documentation: Add an entity to Chariot

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.add.asset
documentation:
Add an asset

Add an asset to the Chariot database. This command requires a DNS name for the asset.
Optionally, a name can be provided to give the asset more specific information,
such as IP address. If no name is provided, the DNS name will be used as the name.

 
Example assets:
    - A domain name:   reddit.com
    - An IP addresses: 208.67.222.222
    - A CIDR range:    208.67.222.0/24

 
Example usages:
    - praetorian chariot add asset --dns example.com
    - praetorian chariot add asset --dns example.com --name 1.2.3.4
    - praetorian chariot add asset --dns example.com --name 1.2.3.4 --priority comprehensive
    - praetorian chariot add asset --dns example.com --name 1.2.3.4 --priority discover

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.add.file
documentation:
Upload a file

This commands takes the path to a local file and uploads it to the
Chariot file system. The Chariot file system is where the platform
stores proofs of exploit, risk definitions, and other supporting data.

User files reside in the "home/" folder. Those files appear in the app
at https://chariot.praetorian.com/app/files

 
Arguments:
    - PATH: the local file path to the file you want to upload.

 
Example usages:
    - praetorian chariot add file ./file.txt
    - praetorian chariot add file ./file.txt --name "home/file.txt"

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.add.definition
documentation:
Upload a risk definition

This commands takes the path to the local file and uploads it to the
Chariot file system as risk definitions. Risk definitions reside
in the "definitions/" folder in the file system.

Risk definitions need to be in the Markdown format.

 
Arguments
    - PATH: the local file path to the risk definition file

 
Example usages:
    - praetorian chariot add definition ./CVE-2024-23049
    - praetorian chariot add definition ./CVE-2024-23049.updated.md --name CVE-2024-23049

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.add.webhook
documentation:
Generate an authenticated URL for posting assets and risks

The command prints the URL of the webhook generated. If the webhook
is already present, you need to first delete it before generating
a new one.

 
Example usages:
    - praetorian chariot add webhook

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.add.risk
documentation:
Add a risk

This command adds a risk to Chariot. A risk must have an associated asset.
The asset is specified by its key, which can be retrieved by listing and
searching the assets.

 
Arguments:
    - NAME: the name of the risk. For example, "CVE-2024-23049".

 
Example usages:
    - praetorian chariot add risk CVE-2024-23049 --asset "#asset#example.com#1.2.3.4" --status TI
    - praetorian chariot add risk CVE-2024-23049 --asset "#asset#example.com#1.2.3.4" --status TC

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.add.job
documentation:
Schedule scan jobs for an asset or an attribute

This command schedules the relevant discovery and vulnerability scans for
the specified asset or attribute. Make sure to quote the key, since it
contains the "#" sign.

 
Example usages:
    - praetorian chariot add job --key "#asset#example.com#1.2.3.4"
    - praetorian chariot add job --key "#asset#example.com#1.2.3.4" -c subdomain -c portscan
    - praetorian chariot add job --key "#attribute#ssh#22#asset#api.www.example.com#1.2.3.4"

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.add.attribute
documentation:
Add an attribute for an asset or risk

This command adds a name-value attribute for the specified asset or risk.

 
Example usages:
    - praetorian chariot add attribute --key "#risk#www.example.com#CVE-2024-23049" --name https --value 443
    - praetorian chariot add attribute --key "#asset#www.example.com#www.example.com" --name id --value "arn:aws:route53::1654874321:hostedzone/Z0000000EJBHGTFTGH3"

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.list.list
documentation: Get a list of entities from Chariot

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.list.assets
documentation:
List assets

    Retrieve and display a list of assets.

 
Example usages:
    - praetorian chariot list assets
    - praetorian chariot list assets --filter api.example.com
    - praetorian chariot list assets --details
    - praetorian chariot list assets --page all

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.list.risks
documentation:
List risks

Retrieve and display a list of risks.

 
Example usages:
    - praetorian chariot list risks
    - praetorian chariot list risks --filter api.example.com
    - praetorian chariot list risks --details
    - praetorian chariot list risks --page all

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.list.accounts
documentation:
List accounts

    Retrieve and display a list of your collaborators, as well as the accounts that
    you are authorized to access.

 
Example usages:
    - praetorian chariot list accounts
    - praetorian chariot list accounts --filter john@praetorian.com
    - praetorian chariot list accounts --details
    - praetorian chariot list accounts --page all

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.list.integrations
documentation:
List integrations

    Retrieve and display a list of integration connections.

 
Example usages:
    - praetorian chariot list integrations
    - praetorian chariot list integrations --filter gcp
    - praetorian chariot list integrations --details
    - praetorian chariot list integrations --page all

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.list.jobs
documentation:
List jobs

Retrieve and display a list of recently scheduled jobs. You can use this
to find out the status of the jobs, such as queued, running, passed, or failed.

 
Example usages:
    - praetorian chariot list jobs
    - praetorian chariot list jobs --filter www.example.com
    - praetorian chariot list jobs --details
    - praetorian chariot list jobs --page all

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.list.files
documentation:
List files

Retrieve and display a list of files in the Chariot file system.

 
Example usages:
    - praetorian chariot list files
    - praetorian chariot list files --filter "home/reports/cloud-assessment-2024-"
    - praetorian chariot list files --details
    - praetorian chariot list files --page all

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.list.definitions
documentation:
List risk definitions

Retrieve and display a list of risk definitions.

 
Example usages:
    - praetorian chariot list definitions
    - praetorian chariot list definitions --filter "home/reports/cloud-assessment-2024-"
    - praetorian chariot list definitions --page all

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.list.attributes
documentation:
List attributes

Retrieve and display a list of attributes.

 
Filtering options:
    - Use the --filter option to filter on the name of the attribute.
    - Use the --key option to filter for attributes of an asset or a risk.
    - You can only filter using either of the above, not together.

 
Example usages:
    - praetorian chariot list attributes
    - praetorian chariot list attributes --filter proto
    - praetorian chariot list attributes --key "#risk#www.example.com#CVE-2024-23049"
    - praetorian chariot list attributes --details
    - praetorian chariot list attributes --page all

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.update.update
documentation: Update an entity in Chariot

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.update.asset
documentation:
Update the priority of an asset

 
Argument:
    - KEY: the key of an existing asset

 
Example usages:
    - praetorian chariot update asset "#asset#www.example.com#1.2.3.4" --priority frozen
    - praetorian chariot update asset "#asset#www.example.com#1.2.3.4" --priority comprehensive

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.update.risk
documentation:
Update the status and comment of a risk

 
Argument:
    - KEY: the key of an existing risk

 
Example usages:
    - praetorian chariot update risk "#risk#www.example.com#CVE-2024-23049" --status OH --comment "Open it as a high severity risk"
    - praetorian chariot update risk "#risk#www.example.com#open-ssh-port" --status RH --comment "John stopped sshd on the server"

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.unlink.unlink
documentation: Remove a collaborator from your account

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.unlink.account
documentation:
Remove a collaborator account from your account. This will
revoke their access to your account.

Arguments:
    - NAME: Their email address.

 
Example usages:
    - praetorian chariot unlink account john@praetorian.com

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.delete.delete
documentation: Delete an entity from Chariot

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.delete.asset
documentation:
Delete an asset

 
Arguments:
    - KEY: the key of an existing asset

 
Example usage:
    - praetorian chariot delete asset "#asset#www.example.com#1.2.3.4"

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.delete.risk
documentation:
Delete a risk

 
Arguments:
    - KEY: the key of an existing risk

 
Example usage:
    - praetorian chariot delete risk "#risk#example.com#CVE-2024-23049"

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.delete.attribute
documentation:
Delete an attribute

 
Arguments:
    - KEY: the key of an existing attribute

 
Example usage:
    - praetorian chariot delete attribute "#attribute#source#kev#risk#api.example.com#CVE-2024-23049"

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.delete.webhook
documentation:
Delete webhook

Example usage:
    - praetorian chariot delete webhook

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.delete.purge
documentation:
Delete account and all related information

Example usage:
    - praetorian chariot purge
Special command for deleting your account and all related information.

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.chariot.chariot
documentation: Command group for interacting with the Chariot product

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.imports.imports
documentation: Import data to Chariot

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.imports.insightvm
documentation:
Import data from Rapid7 InsightVM

The export file needs to be in the XML Export 2.0 format. See details at
https://docs.praetorian.com/hc/en-us/articles/29778072321307-Rapid7-InsightVM

 
Example usages:
    - praetorian chariot import insightvm ~/Downloads/insightvm-report.xml

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.imports.qualys
documentation:
Import data from Qualys VMDR

The export file needs to be in the CSV format. See details at
https://docs.praetorian.com/hc/en-us/articles/29776468370331-Qualys-VMDR

 
Example usages:
    - praetorian chariot import qualys ~/Downloads/qualys-report.csv

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.imports.nessus
documentation:
Import data from Tenable Nessus

The export file needs to be in the Tenable Nessus (.nessus) format. See details at
https://docs.praetorian.com/hc/en-us/articles/28054164449435-Tenable-Nessus

 
Example usages:
    - praetorian chariot import nessus ~/Downloads/nessus-report.nessus

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.configure.configure
documentation: Configure the CLI

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.script.script
documentation: Run a script

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.script.load_dynamic_commands
documentation:
If the PRAETORIAN_SCRIPTS_PATH env variable is defined,
load all the script defined there in those paths.

functions.name: praetorian_cli.handlers.script.load_directory
documentation:
Scan all the Python files in the directory for scripts that are
compatible with the CLI. Files with a register() function will
get called to add a command under the 'script' group.

modules.name: praetorian_cli.scripts.commands.nmap-example
documentation:
For developers:
You can use this as a template for writing new CLI scripts.

How to use:

  1. Install the latest version of praetorian-cli

  2. Set PRAETORIAN_SCRIPTS_PATH environment variable to point to the directory
     where this script is located

  3. Run it:
     `praetorian chariot script --help`
     `praetorian chariot script nmap-example --help`
     `praetorian chariot script nmap-example scanme.nmap.org`

functions.name: praetorian_cli.scripts.commands.nmap-example.nmap_command
documentation:
An nmap script for scanning a host

HOST is the host you want to scan. It can be a hostname or an IP address.
The nmap_command() function is the entry point to the command.
In this example, the command takes one argument from the user, the hostname,
and runs nmap against it to check for open ports in 22, 80, and 443.
The scripting engine passes the arguments and options from your users to this function.
The engine handles the plumbing of Click for you.
Several things you need to follow for this to work:
1. Use @click.command() to register the command name you want.
2. You need to add the @cli_handler decorator.
3. You can use any @click functions for managing the command line arguments and options.
4. The first argument has to be`sdk`. This is the reference to the SDK with an
authenticated Chariot session.
5. The rest of arguments are Click arguments, in order.

functions.name: praetorian_cli.scripts.commands.nmap-example.register
documentation: The register() function has to be defined in this file. It is called by the CLI
to register your command under the script group.

methods.name: praetorian_cli.sdk.keychain.Keychain.headers
documentation: Get the authentication and assume-role headers for backend requests

methods.name: praetorian_cli.sdk.keychain.Keychain.load
documentation: Loads backend and authentication data from the keychain file into this instance.

methods.name: praetorian_cli.sdk.keychain.Keychain.token
documentation: Authenticate to AWS Cognito and get the token. Cache the token until expiry.

methods.name: praetorian_cli.sdk.keychain.Keychain.base_url
documentation: Get the base URL for the backend. It is the "api" field in the keychain file.

methods.name: praetorian_cli.sdk.keychain.Keychain.username
documentation: Get the username field from the keychain profile

methods.name: praetorian_cli.sdk.keychain.Keychain.password
documentation: Get the password field from the keychain profile

methods.name: praetorian_cli.sdk.keychain.Keychain.client_id
documentation: Get the client_id field from the keychain profile

methods.name: praetorian_cli.sdk.keychain.Keychain.assume_role
documentation: Assume into another account

methods.name: praetorian_cli.sdk.keychain.Keychain.unassume_role
documentation: Resume using the sign-in account as the principal

methods.name: praetorian_cli.sdk.keychain.Keychain.configure
documentation:
Update or insert a new profile to the keychain file at the default location.
If the keychain file does not exist, create it.

classes.name: praetorian_cli.sdk.entities.integrations.Integrations

methods.name: praetorian_cli.sdk.entities.integrations.Integrations.get
documentation: Get details of an integration

methods.name: praetorian_cli.sdk.entities.integrations.Integrations.list
documentation:
List integrations, optionally filtered by the name of the integrations,
such as github, amazon, gcp, etc.

The methods in this class are to be assessed from sdk.integrations, where sdk is an instance
of Chariot.

classes.name: praetorian_cli.sdk.entities.assets.Assets

methods.name: praetorian_cli.sdk.entities.assets.Assets.add
documentation:
Add an asset

Arguments:
dns: str
    The DNS name of the asset
name: str
    The name of the asset
status: str
    See globals.py for list of valid statuses

methods.name: praetorian_cli.sdk.entities.assets.Assets.get
documentation:
Get details of an asset

Arguments:
key: str
    The exact key of an asset.
details: bool
    Specify whether to also retrieve more details with this asset. This will
    make more API calls for the asset attributes and the associated risks.

methods.name: praetorian_cli.sdk.entities.assets.Assets.update
documentation:
Update an asset; only status field makes sense to be updated.

Arguments:
key: str
    The key of an asset. If you supply a prefix that matches multiple assets,
    all of them will be updated.
status: str
    See globals.py for list of valid statuses

methods.name: praetorian_cli.sdk.entities.assets.Assets.delete
documentation:
Delete an asset

Arguments:
key: str
    The key of an asset. If you supply a prefix that matches multiple assets,
    all of them will be deleted.

methods.name: praetorian_cli.sdk.entities.assets.Assets.list
documentation:
List assets

Arguments:
prefix_filter: str
    Supply this to perform prefix-filtering of the asset keys after the "#asset#"
    portion of the asset key. Asset keys read '#asset#{dns}#{name}'
offset: str
    The offset of the page you want to retrieve results. If this is not supplied,
    this function retrieves from the first page.
pages: int
    The number of pages of results to retrieve.

methods.name: praetorian_cli.sdk.entities.assets.Assets.attributes
documentation: list associated attributes

The methods in this class are to be assessed from sdk.assets, where sdk is an instance
of Chariot.

classes.name: praetorian_cli.sdk.entities.risks.Risks

methods.name: praetorian_cli.sdk.entities.risks.Risks.add
documentation:
Add a risk

Arguments:
asset_key:
    The key of an existing asset to associate this risk with
name: str
    The name of this risk
status: str
    See globals.py for list of valid statuses
comment: str
    Optional comment

methods.name: praetorian_cli.sdk.entities.risks.Risks.get
documentation:
Get details of a risk

Arguments:
key: str
    The exact key of an risk.
details: bool
    Specify whether to also retrieve more details about this risk. This will make more API calls
    to get the risk attributes and the affected assets.

methods.name: praetorian_cli.sdk.entities.risks.Risks.update
documentation:
Update a risk

Arguments:
key: str
    The key of the risk. If you supply a prefix that matches multiple risks,
    all of them will be updated.
status: str
    See globals.py for list of valid statuses
comment: str
    Comment for the risk

methods.name: praetorian_cli.sdk.entities.risks.Risks.delete
documentation:
Delete a risk.

Arguments:
key: str
    The key of the risk. If you supply a prefix that matches multiple risks,
    all of them will be deleted.
comment: str
    Optionally, provide a comment for this operation.

methods.name: praetorian_cli.sdk.entities.risks.Risks.list
documentation:
List risks

Arguments:
prefix_filter: str
    Supply this to perform prefix-filtering of the risk keys after the "#risk#"
    portion of the key. Risk keys read '#risk#{asset_dns}#{risk_name}'

offset: str
    The offset of the page you want to retrieve results. If this is not supplied,
    this function retrieves from the first page.
pages: int
    The number of pages of results to retrieve.

methods.name: praetorian_cli.sdk.entities.risks.Risks.attributes
documentation: list associated attributes

The methods in this class are to be assessed from sdk.risks, where sdk is an instance
of Chariot.

classes.name: praetorian_cli.sdk.entities.accounts.Accounts

methods.name: praetorian_cli.sdk.entities.accounts.Accounts.get
documentation: Get details of an account

methods.name: praetorian_cli.sdk.entities.accounts.Accounts.list
documentation:
List accounts of collaborators and also list the master accounts that the current
principal can access.

Optionally filtered by username of the collaborators or the authorized accounts.

methods.name: praetorian_cli.sdk.entities.accounts.Accounts.add_collaborator
documentation: Add a collaborator to the account of the current principal

methods.name: praetorian_cli.sdk.entities.accounts.Accounts.delete_collaborator
documentation: Delete a collaborator of the account of the current principal

methods.name: praetorian_cli.sdk.entities.accounts.Accounts.collaborators
documentation:
return emails of all users that are collaborating with the current
principal. The current principal can be an assume-role account.

methods.name: praetorian_cli.sdk.entities.accounts.Accounts.authorized_accounts
documentation:
return emails of all users that the current principal is authorized to access.
The current principal can be an assume-role account.

methods.name: praetorian_cli.sdk.entities.accounts.Accounts.assume_role
documentation: Switch session the assume-role account

methods.name: praetorian_cli.sdk.entities.accounts.Accounts.unassume_role
documentation: Switch back to the login principal

methods.name: praetorian_cli.sdk.entities.accounts.Accounts.current_principal
documentation: Tell you which account the current session is operating on

methods.name: praetorian_cli.sdk.entities.accounts.Accounts.login_principal
documentation:
Tell you the user account that is used to login, regardless of who the current
assume-role account is

The methods in this class are to be assessed from sdk.accounts, where sdk is an instance
of Chariot.
